lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Tell me, good grandam, is our father dead?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: No, boy.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Why do you wring your hands, and beat your breast,

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: And cry “O Clarence, my unhappy son!”

lines.speaker: GIRL
lines.text: Why do you look on us, and shake your head,

lines.speaker: GIRL
lines.text: And call us wretches, orphans, castaways,

lines.speaker: GIRL
lines.text: If that our noble father be alive?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: My pretty cousins, you mistake me much;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I do lament the sickness of the king,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: As loath to lose him, not your father’s death;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: It were lost sorrow to wail one that’s lost.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Then, grandam, you conclude that he is dead.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: The king my uncle is to blame for this:

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: God will revenge it; whom I will importune

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: With daily prayers all to that effect.

lines.speaker: GIRL
lines.text: And so will I.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Peace, children, peace! the king doth love you well:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Incapable and shallow innocents,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: You cannot guess who caused your father’s death.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Grandam, we can; for my good uncle Gloucester

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Told me, the king, provoked by the queen,

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Devised impeachments to imprison him:

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: And when my uncle told me so, he wept,

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: And hugg’d me in his arm, and kindly kiss’d my cheek;

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Bade me rely on him as on my father,

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: And he would love me dearly as his child.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Oh, that deceit should steal such gentle shapes,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And with a virtuous vizard hide foul guile!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: He is my son; yea, and therein my shame;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Yet from my dugs he drew not this deceit.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Think you my uncle did dissemble, grandam?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Ay, boy.

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: I cannot think it. Hark! what noise is this?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Oh, who shall hinder me to wail and weep,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To chide my fortune, and torment myself?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I’ll join with black despair against my soul,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And to myself become an enemy.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: What means this scene of rude impatience?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To make an act of tragic violence:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Edward, my lord, your son, our king, is dead.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Why grow the branches now the root is wither’d?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Why wither not the leaves the sap being gone?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If you will live, lament; if die, be brief,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That our swift-winged souls may catch the king’s;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Or, like obedient subjects, follow him

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To his new kingdom of perpetual rest.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Ah, so much interest have I in thy sorrow

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: As I had title in thy noble husband!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I have bewept a worthy husband’s death,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And lived by looking on his images:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: But now two mirrors of his princely semblance

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Are crack’d in pieces by malignant death,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And I for comfort have but one false glass,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Which grieves me when I see my shame in him.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Thou art a widow; yet thou art a mother,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And hast the comfort of thy children left thee:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: But death hath snatch’d my husband from mine arms,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And pluck’d two crutches from my feeble limbs,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Edward and Clarence. O, what cause have I,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Thine being but a moiety of my grief,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: To overgo thy plaints and drown thy cries!

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: Good aunt, you wept not for our father’s death;

lines.speaker: BOY
lines.text: How can we aid you with our kindred tears?

lines.speaker: GIRL
lines.text: Our fatherless distress was left unmoan’d;

lines.speaker: GIRL
lines.text: Your widow-dolour likewise be unwept!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Give me no help in lamentation;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I am not barren to bring forth complaints:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: All springs reduce their currents to mine eyes,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That I, being govern’d by the watery moon,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: May send forth plenteous tears to drown the world!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Oh for my husband, for my dear lord Edward!

lines.speaker: CHILDREN
lines.text: Oh for our father, for our dear lord Clarence!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Alas for both, both mine, Edward and Clarence!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: What stay had I but Edward? and he’s gone.

lines.speaker: CHILDREN
lines.text: What stay had we but Clarence? and he’s gone.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: What stays had I but they? and they are gone.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Was never widow had so dear a loss!

lines.speaker: CHILDREN
lines.text: Were never orphans had so dear a loss!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Was never mother had so dear a loss!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Alas, I am the mother of these moans!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Their woes are parcell’d, mine are general.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: She for an Edward weeps, and so do I;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I for a Clarence weep, so doth not she:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: These babes for Clarence weep, and so do I;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I for an Edward weep, so do not they:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Alas, you three, on me, threefold distress’d,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Pour all your tears! I am your sorrow’s nurse,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And I will pamper it with lamentations.

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: Comfort, dear mother: God is much displeased

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: That you take with unthankfulness his doing:

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: In common worldly things, ’tis call’d ungrateful,

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: With dull unwilligness to repay a debt

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: Which with a bounteous hand was kindly lent;

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: Much more to be thus opposite with heaven,

lines.speaker: DORSET
lines.text: For it requires the royal debt it lent you.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Madam, bethink you, like a careful mother,

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Of the young prince your son: send straight for him;

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Let him be crown’d; in him your comfort lives:

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Drown desperate sorrow in dead Edward’s grave,

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: And plant your joys in living Edward’s throne.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Madam, have comfort: all of us have cause

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To wail the dimming of our shining star;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But none can cure their harms by wailing them.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Madam, my mother, I do cry you mercy;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I did not see your grace: humbly on my knee

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I crave your blessing.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: God bless thee; and put meekness in thy mind,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Love, charity, obedience, and true duty!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Amen; and make me die a good old man!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That is the butt-end of a mother’s blessing:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I marvel why her grace did leave it out.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: You cloudy princes and heart-sorrowing peers,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: That bear this mutual heavy load of moan,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Now cheer each other in each other’s love:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Though we have spent our harvest of this king,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: We are to reap the harvest of his son.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The broken rancour of your high-swoln hearts,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But lately splinter’d, knit, and join’d together,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Must gently be preserved, cherish’d, and kept:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Me seemeth good, that, with some little train,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Forthwith from Ludlow the young prince be fetch’d

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Hither to London, to be crown’d our king.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Why with some little train, my Lord of Buckingham?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Marry, my lord, lest, by a multitude,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: The new-heal’d wound of malice should break out;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Which would be so much the more dangerous,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: By how much the estate is green and yet ungovern’d:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Where every horse bears his commanding rein,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And may direct his course as please himself,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: As well the fear of harm, as harm apparent,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: In my opinion, ought to be prevented.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I hope the king made peace with all of us;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And the compact is firm and true in me.

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: And so in me; and so, I think, in all:

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Yet, since it is but green, it should be put

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: To no apparent likelihood of breach,

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Which haply by much company might be urged:

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: Therefore I say with noble Buckingham,

lines.speaker: RIVERS
lines.text: That it is meet so few should fetch the prince.

lines.speaker: HASTINGS
lines.text: And so say I.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Then be it so; and go we to determine

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Who they shall be that straight shall post to Ludlow.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Madam, and you, my mother, will you go

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To give your censures in this weighty business?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH DUCHESS
lines.text: With all our hearts.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: My lord, whoever journeys to the prince.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: For God’s sake, let not us two be behind;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: For, by the way, I’ll sort occasion,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: As index to the story we late talk’d of,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To part the queen’s proud kindred from the king.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My other self, my counsel’s consistory,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My oracle, my prophet! My dear cousin,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I, like a child, will go by thy direction.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Towards Ludlow then, for we’ll not stay behind.

stage_directions.text: Enter the Duchess of York, with the two children of Clarence.
Enter Queen Elizabeth, with her hair about her ears; Rivers, and Dorset after her.
Enter Gloucester, Buckingham, Derby, Hastings, and Ratcliff.
Exeunt all but Buckingham and Gloucester.

glosses.definition: grandmother — here the Duchess of York
relatives generally — the Duchess addresses her grandchildren
wasted grief — grief for one already gone can't bring them back
formal accusations of treason

glosses.phrase: deceit should steal such gentle shapes
glosses.definition: that treachery should borrow such gentle appearances
virtuous mask — vizard = visor/mask

glosses.phrase: from my dugs he drew not this deceit
glosses.definition: he did not nurse this treachery from my breasts — the Duchess distances herself from Richard's evil
theological despair — the sin that precludes salvation (cf. 1.2.84 and the ghost scene)

glosses.phrase: Why grow the branches now the root is wither’d
glosses.definition: Elizabeth's formal arboreal lament — why does life continue when its source is dead?
heaven; the afterlife

glosses.phrase: two mirrors of his princely semblance
glosses.definition: Edward IV and Clarence — each reflecting the father York
one deceiving mirror — Richard, the third son, who falsely reflects his father
half; portion — the Duchess claims greater grief
exceed thy lamentations
widow's sorrow — 'dolour' = pain
ruled by the moon (which governs tides) — tears as tide-water
their sorrows are partial; mine are whole
nurse to sorrow — the Duchess frames herself as grief's foster-mother
Dorset's rebuke — the queen's son urges Christian resignation
heaven lent Edward's life and now calls in the loan
the stock-end of a weapon; the last part — Richard's aside on his mother's blessing
Buckingham's image — reaping the new king's bounty
splinted; held together with splints (the surgeon's term)
Ludlow Castle, Shropshire — Prince Edward's household was established there
the state is new, unseasoned
table-of-contents of the story — Buckingham's metaphor for their earlier plotting
my counsel's assembly — 'consistory' = ecclesiastical court; Richard elevates Buckingham's status
Richard's flattery of Buckingham — the religious vocabulary foreshadows 3.7's staged piety

annotations.title: The first formation of the wailing-queens pattern
annotations.body: This is the play's first great formal lament, and it rehearses the set-piece that will reach full scale in 4.4. Queen Elizabeth, the Duchess of York, and Clarence's two children (Boy and Girl) arrange themselves in a pattern of antiphonal grief: each names a dead kinsman, then each responds with parallel syntax. "Was never widow had so dear a loss!" / "Were never orphans had so dear a loss!" / "Was never mother had so dear a loss!" The pattern is not decorative — it is the scene's architecture, and it tells the audience how to read grief in this play. Grief is not individual but choric. The women and children of the dynasty collectively mourn a political history their male kin built, and the formal symmetry enacts the claim that their sorrow is equal and shared. Shakespeare will tighten and extend the pattern in 4.4, adding Margaret; the pattern here prepares that reach.
sources: Jean Howard and Phyllis Rackin, Engendering a Nation (1997)

annotations.title: The Duchess of York's terrible knowledge
annotations.body: The Duchess's aside about Richard — "He is my son; yea, and therein my shame; / Yet from my dugs he drew not this deceit" — is the play's first frank maternal condemnation of Richard. She is saying he is her son biologically but not morally; she did not nurse him into his villainy. The line is important because Margaret's later insults will accuse the Duchess of precisely this — of having hatched "a hell-hound" in her womb (4.4.47) — and the Duchess will accept that judgement. Here she is still trying to separate herself from him. The theological vocabulary ("shame," "deceit") and the emphasis on nursing ("dugs") set up the play's peculiar pressure on mothers: in Richard III, maternal love is the one bond Richard's rhetoric can only reach by parody.
sources: Coppélia Kahn, Man's Estate: Masculine Identity in Shakespeare (1981)

annotations.title: Buckingham moves the plot — the trip to Ludlow
annotations.body: The scene's political hinge is Buckingham's proposal that the young prince be fetched from Ludlow to London with "some little train" — a minimal escort. The reasoning given is public-order prudence (a large retinue would revive faction); the real purpose, announced to Richard in aside, is to "part the queen's proud kindred from the king." This is the play's first clear demonstration that Buckingham is not merely a witness to Richard's plotting but an equal designer. Richard's response — "My other self, my counsel's consistory, / My oracle, my prophet!" — is the most extravagant flattery he delivers to any character in the play, and the reader should notice it. In 3 Henry VI Richard had no such partner. Buckingham is new. His usefulness to Richard extends only as long as his agreement; when he hesitates in 4.2 over the princes' murder, the flattery-vocabulary evaporates and he is marked for death.
sources: Charles R. Forker, "Webster's Prose and the Rhetoric of Despair" (1977)

annotations.title: Dorset's Christian resignation and what the play does with it
annotations.body: The Marquess of Dorset's rebuke to his grieving mother — "God is much displeased / That you take with unthankfulness his doing" — is the voice of orthodox Elizabethan piety. Death is a debt; complaint against God's taking is ingratitude. The speech is sincere, and it is also dramatically insufficient. The play does not quarrel with the theology but demonstrates its limits: Dorset will himself be in exile by Act IV (fleeing to Richmond), and the "royal debt" language he deploys will by 4.4 be revealed as a mask for the plot's actual mechanism, which is not God's taking but Richard's. Shakespeare is careful not to reject the theology outright. He lets it stand, and lets its inadequacy to the play's suffering reveal itself as the plot continues. Consolation is real in Richard III; it is simply not sufficient.
sources: Debora K. Shuger, Habits of Thought in the English Renaissance (1990)
Huston Diehl, Staging Reform, Reforming the Stage (1997)

trials.prompt: The formally parallel exchange ("Was never widow had so dear a loss! / Were never orphans had so dear a loss! / Was never mother had so dear a loss!") serves what function?
options: Ornamental rhetoric without structural purpose
The scene's architecture — it enacts the claim that grief in this play is choric and collectively shared
A parody of orthodox prayer
A riddle whose answer the audience must infer

trials.prompt: The Duchess of York, speaking of Richard, says "He is my son... Yet from my dugs he drew not this deceit." What does she mean?
options: That he is not in fact her biological son
That he is her biological son but she did not nurse him into his villainy — she separates herself morally from his acts
That she refused to breastfeed him, and this is the reason for his character
That he was raised by a wet-nurse rather than by her

trials.prompt: Why does Buckingham want the young prince fetched from Ludlow with "some little train"?
options: To save expense
The stated reason is public order; the private reason (announced to Richard in aside) is to separate the prince from the queen's "proud kindred"
To speed the journey back to London
To avoid attracting the notice of foreign ambassadors

trials.prompt: Richard greets Buckingham as "My other self, my counsel's consistory, / My oracle, my prophet!" What does this flattery signal?
options: Sincere religious devotion
That Buckingham is Richard's equal partner in design at this moment — a relationship that will hold only until Buckingham hesitates in 4.2
That Richard has converted to Catholic piety
A casual formulaic courtesy, with no dramatic weight

trials.prompt: Dorset's counsel to his mother — "God is much displeased that you take with unthankfulness his doing" — represents what?
options: Explicit blasphemy
Orthodox Elizabethan Christian consolation, which the play respects but ultimately finds insufficient to its suffering
A sarcastic mockery of piety
A legal argument against inheritance disputes
